519	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
520	
521	\begin{lemma}
522	
523	\leanok
524	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
525	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
527	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
528	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
529	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
530	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
531	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
532	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
533	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
534	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
535	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
536	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
537	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
538	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
539	  % $$
540	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
541	  % $$
542	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
543	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
544	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
545	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
546	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
547	  % we have
548	  % $$
549	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
550	  % $$
551	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
552	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
553	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
554	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
555	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
556	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
557	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
558	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
559	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
560	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
561	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
562	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
563	  \[
564	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
565	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
566	  \]
567	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
568	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
569	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
570	
571	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
572	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
573	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
574	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
575	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
576	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
577	  direct image of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} against the
578	  derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
579	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
580	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
581	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
582	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
583	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
584	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
585	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
586	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
587	  role in the applications.
588	\end{lemma}
589	
590	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In view of
591	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
592	% this is a special case of
593	% Cohomology, Lemma
594	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
595	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
596	% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
597	% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
598	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
599	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
600	% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
601	% By Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
602	% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
603	% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
604	\begin{proof}
605	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
606	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
607	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
608	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
609	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
610	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
611	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
612	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
613	
614	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
615	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
616	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
617	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
618	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
619	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
620	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
621	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
622	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
623	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
624	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
625	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).
626	
627	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
628	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
629	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
630	  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
631	  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
632	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
633	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
634	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
635	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
636	
637	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
638	  infrastructure: the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and the Leray
639	  spectral sequence for the category of sheaves of modules on a scheme, neither of
640	  which is presently available there; the argument uses both to collapse onto the
641	  comparison isomorphism.}
642	\end{proof}
643	
644	\section{The unconditional higher direct image}
645	
646	\begin{definition}
647	
648	\leanok
649	[Unconditional higher direct image via {\v C}ech]
650	  \label{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
651	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage}
652	  \uses{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
653	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
654	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
655	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L843--854); the unconditional
656	  % packaging via the {\v C}ech complex is an Archon-original construction grounded
657	  % in this lemma and Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
658	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
659	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
660	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
661	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
662	  % we have
663	  % $$
664	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
665	  % $$
666	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
667	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
668	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
669	  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
670	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
671	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
672	  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
673	  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
674	  relative {\v C}ech complex,
675	  \[
676	    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
677	      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
678	      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
679	  \]
680	  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
681	  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
682	  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
683	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
684	  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
685	  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
686	  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
687	  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
688	  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
689	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
690	  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
691	\end{definition}
692	
693	\section{Flat base change for the {\v C}ech higher direct images}
694	
695	\begin{lemma}
696	
697	\leanok
698	[Flat base change of $R^i f_*$ via {\v C}ech]
699	  \label{lem:cech_flat_base_change}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_flatBaseChange}
701	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
702	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH (Lemma
703	  % \texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, "Flat base change")
704	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970).
705	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
706	  % $$
707	  % \xymatrix{
708	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
709	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
710	  % }
711	  % $$
712	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
713	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
714	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
715	  % For any $i \geq 0$
716	  % \begin{enumerate}
717	  % \item the base change map of
718	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
719	  % is an isomorphism
720	  % $$
721	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
722	  % $$
723	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
724	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
725	  % \end{enumerate}"
726	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH
727	  (\texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, ``Flat base change'').}
728	  Consider the cartesian square
729	  \[
730	    \begin{array}{ccc}
731	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
732	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
733	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
734	    \end{array}
735	  \]
736	  with \(f : X \to S\) separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
737	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and
738	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume \(g\) is flat. Then for every
739	  \(i \geq 0\) the canonical base-change map between the unconditional {\v C}ech
740	  higher direct images of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} is an
741	  isomorphism
742	  \[
743	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
744	      R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'.
745	  \]
746	  Equivalently, when \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
747	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
748	  \(B\)-modules \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an
749	  isomorphism.
750	\end{lemma}
751	
752	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
753	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
754	% and $S'$ are affine. ...
755	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
756	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
757	% $$
758	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
759	% $$
760	% see
761	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
762	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
763	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
764	% and that $X_B$ is separated. ...
765	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
766	% $$
767	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
768	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
769	% $$
770	% as follows from
771	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
772	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
773	\begin{proof}
774	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
775	  The assertion that the base-change map is an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we
776	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
777	  \(A \to B\) flat. By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} both
778	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) and \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) are quasi-coherent, associated
779	  to the \(A\)-module \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\) and the \(B\)-module
780	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) respectively; since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
781	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, it suffices to prove that the comparison map
782	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an isomorphism.
783	
784	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\);
785	  by separatedness all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) are affine. Base
786	  changing the cover yields an affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U}_B\) of the
787	  again-separated \(X' = X_B\), with each \((U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_B\) affine. By
788	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the two {\v C}ech complexes compute the
789	  respective cohomologies. The affine base change for the \(i = 0\) direct image
790	  identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term
791	  tensored over \(A\) with \(B\), giving an isomorphism of cochain complexes
792	  \[
793	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
794	      \;\cong\;
795	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B.
796	  \]
797	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and commutes with the
798	  formation of cohomology of a complex; applying \(H^i\) for any \(i \geq 0\) yields
799	  \[
800	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
801	      H^i(X', \mathcal{F}').
802	  \]
803	  Translating back to sheaves on \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) — using that the
804	  unconditional higher direct images are the quasi-coherent sheaves associated to
805	  these modules — gives the asserted isomorphism
806	  \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) for all
807	  \(i \geq 0\). The flatness of \(g\) (hence of \(g'\), by base change) is exactly
808	  what makes \(- \otimes_A B\) exact, and is therefore the precise hypothesis under
809	  which the {\v C}ech computation of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
810	  is compatible with base change.
811	
812	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
813	  infrastructure: the term-wise affine base change of the {\v C}ech complex
814	  identifying \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \cong
815	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\), and the
816	  exactness of \(- \otimes_A B\) (its commutation with the formation of cohomology
817	  of a complex) for the relevant categories of sheaves of modules, neither of which
818	  is presently available there.}
819	\end{proof}
820

{"symbol":"have hru := Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_right_unitality (X := Y.left) (f := Y.hom)","info":"The `have` tactic is for adding opaque definitions and hypotheses to the local context of the main goal.
The definitions forget their associated value and cannot be unfolded, unlike definitions added by the `let` tactic.

* `have h : t := e` adds the hypothesis `h : t` if `e` is a term of type `t`.
* `have h := e` uses the type of `e` for `t`.
* `have : t := e` and `have := e` use `this` for the name of the hypothesis.
* `have pat := e` for a pattern `pat` is equivalent to `match e with | pat => _`,
  where `_` stands for the tactics that follow this one.
  It is convenient for types that have only one applicable constructor.
  For example, given `h : p ∧ q ∧ r`, `have ⟨h₁, h₂, h₃⟩ := h` produces the
  hypotheses `h₁ : p`, `h₂ : q`, and `h₃ : r`.
* The syntax `have (eq := h) pat := e` is equivalent to `match h : e with | pat => _`,
  which adds the equation `h : e = pat` to the local context.

The tactic supports all the same syntax variants and options as the `have` term.

## Properties and relations

* It is not possible to unfold a variable introduced using `have`, since the definition's value is forgotten.
  The `let` tactic introduces definitions that can be unfolded.
* The `have h : t := e` is like doing `let h : t := e; clear_value h`.
* The `have` tactic is preferred for propositions, and `let` is preferred for non-propositions.
* Sometimes `have` is used for non-propositions to ensure that the variable is never unfolded,
  which may be important for performance reasons.
    Consider using the equivalent `let +nondep` to indicate the intent.","diagnostics":[]}

cat > /tmp/pushpull_prompt.md << 'EOF'
I am formalizing in Lean 4 / Mathlib the functoriality (composition law) of a "push-pull" functor on sheaves of modules over schemes. I need a precise proof strategy.

# Definitions (AlgebraicGeometry, sheaves of O_X-modules; `Scheme.Modules`)

For `f : X ⟶ Y` of schemes, Mathlib provides:
- `Scheme.Modules.pullback f : X.Modules ⥤ Y.Modules`  (note: contravariant-looking; pullback f : X.Modules ⥤ Y.Modules where f : X ⟶ Y... actually pullback f : (codomain).Modules ⥤ (domain).Modules)
- `Scheme.Modules.pushforward f : (domain).Modules ⥤ (codomain).Modules`
- adjunction `pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f : pullback f ⊣ pushforward f`
- iso `pushforwardComp f g : pushforward f ⋙ pushforward g ≅ pushforward (f ≫ g)`
- iso `pullbackComp f g : pullback g ⋙ pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g)`
- `pushforwardId X : pushforward (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭`, `pullbackId X : pullback (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭`

KEY: `pushforwardComp f g .hom.app M` is SECTIONWISE THE IDENTITY (its value at every open U is `𝟙`). Same for `pushforwardId`. So any equation among pushforward-comparison composites is closed by `hom_ext; intro U; simp [pushforwardComp_hom_app_app, pushforwardId_hom_app_app]` / `rfl`.

Coherence lemmas available (Mathlib):
- `unit_conjugateEquiv (adj₁ : L₁⊣R₁) (adj₂ : L₂⊣R₂) (α : L₂⟶L₁) (c) : adj₁.unit.app c ≫ (conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂ α).app (L₁.obj c) = adj₂.unit.app c ≫ R₂.map (α.app c)`
- `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv f g : conjugateEquiv ((adj g).comp (adj f)) (adj (f≫g)) (pullbackComp f g).inv = (pushforwardComp f g).hom`
- `Adjunction.comp_unit_app (adj₁:F⊣G)(adj₂:H⊣I)(X) : (adj₁.comp adj₂).unit.app X = adj₁.unit.app X ≫ G.map (adj₂.unit.app (F.obj X))`
- `pseudofunctor_associativity (f)(g)(h) : (pullbackComp f (g≫h)).inv ≫ whiskerRight (pullbackComp g h).inv (pullback f) ≫ (associator ...).hom ≫ whiskerLeft (pullback h) (pullbackComp f g).hom ≫ (pullbackComp (f≫g) h).hom = eqToHom ...`
- `Adjunction.unit_naturality`

# The push-pull data

For a quasi-coherent F : X.Modules and an X-scheme `(Y, p : Y.left ⟶ X)` (object of `Over X`):
`pushPullObj F Y := (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((pullback Y.hom).obj F)`  (i.e. p_* p^* F)

For a morphism `k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁` in `Over X` (so `Over.w k : k.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`), with p₁=Y₁.hom, p₂=Y₂.hom, ḡ=k.left, M=(pullback p₁).obj F:
```
pushPullMap F k : pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
  (pushforward p₁).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction ḡ).unit.app M)
  ≫ (pushforwardComp ḡ p₁).hom.app ((pullback ḡ).obj M)
  ≫ eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj ((pullback ḡ).obj M)) (Over.w k))
  ≫ (pushforward p₂).map ((pullbackComp ḡ p₁).hom.app F)
  ≫ eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward p₂).obj ((pullback q).obj F)) (Over.w k))
```

# GOAL

Prove the contravariant composition law: for `g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂`, `h : Y₂ ⟶ Y₃` in `Over X`:
```
pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g
```
The identity law `pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙` is already proven via: `star := unit_conjugateEquiv (Adjunction.id) (adj (𝟙 Y.left)) (pullbackId Y.left).hom M` rewritten by `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom`; `hpf` collapsing pushforwardComp(𝟙,_) glue by `hom_ext;intro U;rfl`; `hib` collapsing the pullbackComp(𝟙,_) glue via `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`; a unit zig-zag `hzig`; then assemble.

Please give a DETAILED Lean 4 tactic proof strategy for the COMPOSITION law, mirroring the identity proof one level up but using `pseudofunctor_associativity` (pullback pentagon), `Adjunction.comp_unit_app`, and `Adjunction.unit_naturality`. Identify:
1. The exact sequence of `have` lemmas (state each precisely).
2. How the eqToHom transports along `Over.w` are managed.
3. How `pushforwardComp(g.left ≫ h.left, p₃)` relates to the iterated pushforward comparisons (sectionwise rfl).
4. The mate-calculus step converting the unit head to conjugate form.
Be concrete about the categorical bookkeeping. This is the crux.

Automation confirms this needs the full manual mate/pentagon calculus — no shortcut, and the hard ingredients already exist in Mathlib (it's assembly bookkeeping). Let me build a genuinely reusable axiom-clean brick: the base-change unit (mate) identity that the pentagon assembly's unit-handling relies on. Let me add it and test.

input.old_string:
/-- Composition law of the push–pull functor `G`. -/
lemma pushPullMap_comp (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Over X}
    (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (h : Y₂ ⟶ Y₃) :
    pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g := by
  sorry
input.new_string:
/-- **Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push–pull head.**
For composable scheme morphisms `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z` and `N : Z.Modules`, the
adjunction unit at `N` for `p` followed by the *head* of `pushPullMap` (the
pushforward of the unit for `f`, then the pushforward comparison) equals the unit
for the composite `f ≫ p` followed by the pushforward of the inverse pullback
comparison. This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
`η^{f≫p}` into the iterated units `η^p`, `η^f`; it is the reusable ingredient that
the functoriality (pentagon) law of `pushPullMap` repeatedly consumes when
splitting a composite unit. Project-local supplement. -/
lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
    (N : Z.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key

/-- Composition law of the push–pull functor `G`. -/

    (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (h : Y₂ ⟶ Y₃) :
    pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g := by
  sorry

/-- Composition law of the push–pull functor `G`. -/
lemma pushPullMap_comp (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Over X}

input.new_string:
/-- **Augmentation naturality of the push–pull functor.** The canonical unit map
`F ⟶ pushPullObj F Y = Y.hom_* Y.hom^* F` (the adjunction unit at `F`) is natural
in the `X`-scheme `Y`: precomposing with `pushPullMap F k` for `k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁`
recovers the unit at `Y₂`. This is the compatibility that makes the constant
functor `F` an augmentation of the cosimplicial object `G ∘ (backbone)`; it is the
degree-`(-1)` coface relation of the {\v C}ech nerve. Project-local supplement;
consumes `pushPull_unit_mate`. -/
lemma pushPullMap_unit (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction Y₁.hom).unit.app F ≫
        pushPullMap F k =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction Y₂.hom).unit.app F := by
  rw [pushPullMap, ← Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc,
    ← Category.assoc, pushPull_unit_mate]
  sorry

Explanation: The rewrite tactic rewrites an expression 'e' using an equality 'a = b' by the following process. First, it looks for all 'a' in 'e'. Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Possible solutions: use rewrite's 'occs' configuration option to limit which occurrences are rewritten, or use 'simp' or 'conv' mode, which have strategies for certain kinds of dependencies (these tactics can handle proofs and 'Decidable' instances whose types depend on the rewritten term, and 'simp' can apply user-defined '@[congr]' theorems as well).

input.old_string: /-- **Augmentation naturality of the push–pull functor.** The canonical unit map
input.new_string:
/-- Transport of the pull–push adjunction unit along an equality of structure
morphisms. Project-local helper for the eqToHom bookkeeping along the over-triangle. -/
private lemma pushforward_unit_eqToHom {B Z : Scheme.{u}} {p q : B ⟶ Z} (e : p = q)
    (N : Z.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
        eqToHom (by rw [e]) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction q).unit.app N := by
  subst e; simp

288	
289	/-- **Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push–pull head.**
290	For composable scheme morphisms `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z` and `N : Z.Modules`, the
291	adjunction unit at `N` for `p` followed by the *head* of `pushPullMap` (the
292	pushforward of the unit for `f`, then the pushforward comparison) equals the unit
293	for the composite `f ≫ p` followed by the pushforward of the inverse pullback
294	comparison. This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
295	`η^{f≫p}` into the iterated units `η^p`, `η^f`; it is the reusable ingredient that
296	the functoriality (pentagon) law of `pushPullMap` repeatedly consumes when
297	splitting a composite unit. Project-local supplement. -/
298	lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)

/-- Transport of the pull–push adjunction unit along an equality of structure
morphisms. Project-local helper for the eqToHom bookkeeping along the over-triangle. -/
private lemma pushforward_unit_eqToHom {B Z : Scheme.{u}} {p q : B ⟶ Z} (e : p = q)
    (N : Z.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
        eqToHom (by rw [e]) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction q).unit.app N := by
  subst e; simp

set_option maxHeartbeats 1000000 in
set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/-- **Augmentation naturality of the push–pull functor.** The canonical unit map
`F ⟶ pushPullObj F Y = Y.hom_* Y.hom^* F` (the adjunction unit at `F`) is natural
in the `X`-scheme `Y`: precomposing with `pushPullMap F k` for `k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁`
recovers the unit at `Y₂`. This is the compatibility that makes the constant
functor `F` an augmentation of the cosimplicial object `G ∘ (backbone)`; it is the
degree-`(-1)` coface relation of the {\v C}ech nerve. Project-local supplement;
consumes `pushPull_unit_mate`. -/
lemma pushPullMap_unit (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction Y₁.hom).unit.app F ≫
        pushPullMap F k =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction Y₂.hom).unit.app F := by
  rw [pushPullMap]
  erw [reassoc_of% (pushPull_unit_mate (Over.Hom.left k) Y₁.hom F)]
  erw [Functor.congr_hom (congrArg Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Over.w k))
    ((pullbackComp (Over.Hom.left k) Y₁.hom).inv.app F)]
  erw [Category.assoc, Category.assoc, eqToHom_trans_assoc, eqToHom_refl, Category.id_comp,
    ← Functor.map_comp_assoc, Iso.inv_hom_id_app, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id,
    Category.id_comp, eqToHom_trans]
  exact pushforward_unit_eqToHom (Over.w k) F

/-- Telescoping of the pull–push comparison along an equality `q = p` of structure
morphisms: the unit for `q`, followed by `q_*` of the inverse comparison `α`, the
`eqToHom` transport `q ↝ p`, `p_*` of `α`, and the second transport, collapses to
the unit for `p`. Proven by `subst` (which turns every `eqToHom` into the identity,
sidestepping the expensive `pushforward`/`pullback` whnf), so it discharges the
over-triangle bookkeeping in `pushPullMap_unit` in a single `exact`. Project-local. -/
private lemma pushPull_unit_telescope {B Z : Scheme.{u}} {q p : B ⟶ Z} (e : q = p)
    (G : Z.Modules ⥤ B.Modules) (α : G ≅ Scheme.Modules.pullback q) (N : Z.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction q).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).map (α.inv.app N) ≫
          eqToHom (congrArg (fun m => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward m).obj (G.obj N)) e) ≫
            (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map (α.hom.app N) ≫
              eqToHom (congrArg
                (fun m => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).obj
                  ((Scheme.Modules.pullback m).obj N)) e) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N := by
  subst e
  simp [← Functor.map_comp, Iso.inv_hom_id_app]

/-- **Augmentation naturality of the push–pull functor.** The canonical unit map
`F ⟶ pushPullObj F Y = Y.hom_* Y.hom^* F` (the adjunction unit at `F`) is natural
in the `X`-scheme `Y`: precomposing with `pushPullMap F k` for `k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁`
recovers the unit at `Y₂`. This is the compatibility that makes the constant
functor `F` an augmentation of the cosimplicial object `G ∘ (backbone)`; it is the
degree-`(-1)` coface relation of the {\v C}ech nerve. Project-local supplement;
consumes `pushPull_unit_mate` and `pushPull_unit_telescope`. -/
lemma pushPullMap_unit (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (k : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction Y₁.hom).unit.app F ≫
        pushPullMap F k =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction Y₂.hom).unit.app F := by
  rw [pushPullMap]
  erw [reassoc_of% (pushPull_unit_mate (Over.Hom.left k) Y₁.hom F)]
  exact pushPull_unit_telescope (Over.w k) _
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (Over.Hom.left k) Y₁.hom) F

# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean

## Summary
- **Declarations added: 1 axiom-clean** — `pushPull_unit_mate` (lines ~298–315).
- **Declarations blocked: 1** — `pushPullMap_comp` (the assigned pentagon law) NOT added.
- **sorry count: 4 → 4** (unchanged: `CechNerve` L89, `CechAcyclic.affine`, `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`,
  `cech_flatBaseChange` — all pre-existing infra-gated). No new sorry, no `pushPullMap_comp` stub left.

## Session summary
- Built axiom-clean: **`pushPull_unit_mate`** — the mate-calculus core of the pentagon. Verified
  `#print axioms` = `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`.
- **Key negative finding (important for the planner):** `pushPullMap_comp` is blocked **not** by the mate
  calculus (which is now a one-liner via `pushPull_unit_mate`) but by a **kernel `whnf` blow-up on the
  `eqToHom` over-triangle transports** baked into the definition of `pushPullMap`.

## `pushPull_unit_mate` (RESOLVED — axiom-clean, line ~298)
- **Statement:** for `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z`, `N : Z.Modules`,
  `η^p.app N ≫ p_*(η^f.app (p^*N)) ≫ (pushforwardComp f p).hom.app _ = η^{f≫p}.app N ≫ (f≫p)_*((pullbackComp f p).inv.app N)`.
- **Approach:** `unit_conjugateEquiv (adj_p.comp adj_f) adj_{f≫p} (pullbackComp f p).inv N`, then
  `rw [conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app]`, then `simpa only [Category.assoc]`.
- **Why it matters:** this is exactly the head-rewrite the pentagon law's unit-splitting needs (turns the
  single-morphism unit `η^{f≫p}` into the iterated `η^p`, `η^f` + the pushforward comparison). Reusable for
  both `pushPullMap_comp` and the Čech-nerve augmentation. **3 lines, fast, kernel-cheap.**

## `pushPullMap_comp` (NOT ADDED — assigned target)
- **Approach 1 (automation):** `simp`/`hom_ext`+sectionwise/`pseudofunctor_associativity` rewrite all FAIL —
  the sectionwise route exposes the full (non-trivial) pullback adjunction unit; the pentagon `rw` needs
  exact pattern shape it never has. Confirmed: no automation shortcut. The hard ingredients
  (`pseudofunctor_associativity`, `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`, `pushforwardComp_hom_app_app = 𝟙`
  sectionwise) ALL exist in Mathlib — the difficulty is pure `eqToHom`-transport assembly bookkeeping, so
  there is no clean "missing Mathlib lemma" to extract.
- **Approach 2 (warm-up `pushPullMap_unit`):** I built the augmentation-naturality lemma
  `η^{Y₁} ≫ pushPullMap F k = η^{Y₂}` as a strictly-easier warm-up that exercises the same machinery.
  It reduces **cleanly and fully** to a `unit ≫ eqToHom = unit` collapse via:
  `rw [pushPullMap]; erw [reassoc_of% (pushPull_unit_mate (k.left) Y₁.hom F)];
   erw [Functor.congr_hom (congrArg pushforward (Over.w k)) ((pullbackComp k.left Y₁.hom).inv.app F)]; …`
  — all the categorical logic works (verified goal states step by step).
- **THE BLOCKER (precise):** the final `eqToHom` cancellation **times out the kernel** (`(kernel)
  deterministic timeout`, and elaboration `whnf` timeout even at `maxHeartbeats 1000000`). Cancelling the
  two over-triangle transports forces the kernel to check `defeq` of `pushforward`/`pullback`-applied
  comparison objects, which `whnf`-explodes. Tried: full `simp` (kernel timeout), surgical
  `erw [eqToHom_trans_assoc, …]` (whnf timeout), `backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false` (no help), and
  a `subst`-based free-variable telescope helper applied by a single `exact` (kernel timeout — the `exact`
  unifies but the kernel can't check the eqToHom defeq). The wall is **intrinsic to the `eqToHom`-heavy
  definition of `pushPullMap`** (the `congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj _) (Over.w g)` transports).
- **Recommended next step (planner):** before re-attempting the pentagon, make `pushPullMap` **transport-light**.
  Either (a) reformulate the def so the over-triangle substitution `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom` is absorbed
  WITHOUT `eqToHom` (e.g. via `Over.w`-aware `Functor.mapIso`/`whisker` that the kernel can reduce, or by
  indexing on the structure map as a bundled equality), or (b) prove a **kernel-cheap `eqToHom`-cancellation
  lemma** for the specific `(pushforward q).map ι ≫ eqToHom ≫ (pushforward p).map π ≫ eqToHom` shape that
  does NOT require whnf-ing pushforward objects (the current `subst`-helper is logically correct but the
  KERNEL still re-checks the eqToHom types). The mate calculus itself (`pushPull_unit_mate`) is done.
- **Dead ends — do NOT retry:** sectionwise `hom_ext` (unit not sectionwise trivial); `rw [Over.w k]`
  (motive not type-correct — pullbackComp depends on the composite); `subst (Over.w k)` (circular — `k`'s
  type ties the variables); raising `maxHeartbeats` (kernel timeout is not heartbeat-bounded for the proof

---
name: ts265-pushpull-comp-kernel-eqtohom-wall
description: CechHigherDirectImage engine — pushPull_unit_mate LANDED; pushPullMap_comp blocked by KERNEL whnf wall on pushPullMap's eqToHom over-triangle transports (NOT the mate calculus)
metadata:
  type: project
---

iter-265 engine lane (`Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean`, `lem:push_pull_functor`).

**LANDED axiom-clean: `pushPull_unit_mate`** (the pentagon's mate core) — `η^p ≫ p_*(η^f) ≫ pushforwardComp.hom = η^{f≫p} ≫ (f≫p)_*(pullbackComp.inv)`. Proof = 3 lines: `unit_conjugateEquiv (adj_p.comp adj_f) adj_{f≫p} (pullbackComp f p).inv N` + `rw [conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app]` + `simpa only [Category.assoc]`. Fast, kernel-cheap, reusable.

**`pushPullMap_comp` (assigned target) NOT closed — but the blocker is NOT what the planner assumed.** The mate calculus is DONE (one-liner via `pushPull_unit_mate`); `pseudofunctor_associativity`/`conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`/`pushforwardComp_hom_app_app=𝟙`(sectionwise) all EXIST in Mathlib ⇒ no missing lemma, just `eqToHom` assembly. **The REAL wall = KERNEL `whnf` blow-up on `pushPullMap`'s over-triangle `eqToHom` transports** (`congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj _) (Over.w g)`). Even the strictly-easier warm-up `pushPullMap_unit` (`η^{Y₁} ≫ pushPullMap F k = η^{Y₂}`) reduces CLEANLY via mate + `Functor.congr_hom (congrArg pushforward (Over.w k))` to `unit ≫ eqToHom = unit`, but the final eqToHom cancel **kernel-times-out** (and elab `whnf` times out at `maxHeartbeats 1000000`): cancelling transports forces kernel defeq-check of `pushforward`/`pullback`-applied objects → exponential whnf.

TRIED & FAILED for the eqToHom cancel: full `simp` (kernel timeout), `erw [eqToHom_trans_assoc,…]` (whnf timeout), `backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false` (no help), `subst`-based free-var telescope helper applied by `exact` (elab unifies, KERNEL still re-checks eqToHom defeq → timeout). DEAD-ENDS: sectionwise `hom_ext` (unit not sectionwise-trivial); `rw [Over.w k]` (motive-not-type-correct, pullbackComp depends on composite); `subst (Over.w k)` (circular — `k`'s type ties the vars); bumping heartbeats (kernel not heartbeat-bounded; whnf fails at 1e6).

USEFUL erw recipes that DID work (for goal building): `erw [reassoc_of% (pushPull_unit_mate (k.left) Y₁.hom F)]` fires the mate (plain `rw`/`slice` FAIL — SheafOfModules defeq-not-syntactic, the recurring wall); `erw [Functor.congr_hom (congrArg pushforward (Over.w k)) (α.inv.app F)]` conjugates `(pushforward p₂).map` back under `pushforward q` (puts both maps under one functor).

**NEXT (planner): make `pushPullMap` transport-LIGHT before re-attempting the pentagon** — reformulate so the `Over.w` substitution is `eqToHom`-free (bundled-equality indexing / `mapIso`), OR a kernel-cheap eqToHom-cancel lemma that avoids whnf-ing pushforward objs. `pushPull_unit_mate` is permanent and feeds the rebuild. Related engine progress: [[ts264-pushpullmap-id-landed]] (the `id` law, which has NO comp eqToHom and so dodged this wall).

Done. Here's a summary of this iteration's work on `Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean`.

## Result

**1 axiom-clean declaration added; assigned target blocked by a precise (and surprising) wall.**

### Landed: `pushPull_unit_mate` (axiom-clean, verified `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`)
The mate-calculus core of the push–pull pentagon — it rewrites the head `p_*(η^f) ≫ pushforwardComp` as `η^{f≫p} ≫ (f≫p)_*(pullbackComp⁻¹)`. Three-line proof via `unit_conjugateEquiv` + `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` + `Adjunction.comp_unit_app`. This is exactly the ingredient the pentagon's unit-splitting consumes, so it's permanent progress toward `pushPullMap_comp`.

### Blocked: `pushPullMap_comp` (the assigned pentagon law) — NOT added (no sorry left)
The important finding: the blocker is **not** the mate calculus the planner anticipated. With `pushPull_unit_mate` the categorical logic reduces cleanly (I verified this on the strictly-easier augmentation-naturality warm-up, which fully reduces to `unit ≫ eqToHom = unit`). The real wall is a **kernel `whnf` blow-up on the `eqToHom` over-triangle transports** baked into `pushPullMap`'s definition: cancelling those transports forces the kernel to unfold `pushforward`/`pullback` on the comparison objects, timing out the kernel even at `maxHeartbeats 1000000`, and even through a `subst`-based free-variable helper applied by a single `exact`.

I documented the precise blocker, the working `erw` recipes, and the dead-ends in `task_results/CechHigherDirectImage.md` and memory. **Recommended next step:** make `pushPullMap` *transport-light* (absorb the `Over.w` substitution without `eqToHom`) before any pentagon attempt — the mate calculus is now done; the eqToHom bookkeeping is the rate-limiter.

The file compiles with no errors and the same 4 pre-existing infra-gated sorries (no new sorry introduced).

The informal-agent key (`MOONSHOT_API_KEY`) is non-functional in this environment (401/404 — scoped to a coding endpoint), which I documented as required.

content: I'll start by reading my role and task files.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
