6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundlePullback
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence
8	
9	/-!
10	# Coherence of locally trivial line bundles (A.2.c engine)
11	
12	This file is the **iter-256 Lane engine** file-skeleton for the cheap, on-path
13	half of the line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme
14	embedding (`chap:Picard_QuotScheme`, `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`).
15	
16	The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a line
17	bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a finitely
18	presented `𝒪`-module. The embedding's input is a point of the relative Picard
19	scheme `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, whose carrier is the **locally trivial** predicate
20	`IsLocallyTrivial` of `chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback` (the project-side stand-in
21	for an invertible sheaf). The chapter proves
22	`IsLocallyTrivial M ⟹ M.IsFinitePresentation`, together with a rank-one /
23	flatness record. The general implication "`M` tensor-invertible `⟹` `M` locally
24	free of rank one" (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma 0B8M when stalks are
25	local) is *not* crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
26	finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
27	
28	## Status (iter-258: 1 sorry → 0 locally; redirected to shared root)
29	
30	The engine file is now **locally `sorry`-free**. The last remaining ingredient,
31	the over↔restrict bridge `chartOverIso`, has been redirected to the general
32	`Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`
33	(the iter-258 shared-root pivot). That general bridge is a complete one-line
34	composite; its remaining mathematical content (the three isos `overEquivalence`,
35	`restrictOverIso`, `unitOverIso`) lives in the shared-root file, where the dual
36	lane (`DualInverse.lean`) consumes the same construction. Once those three close,
37	this engine becomes fully axiom-clean with no further edits here.
38	
39	Of the five pinned declarations, four were closed axiom-clean and the fifth
40	(`chartPresentation`) is complete modulo the (now redirected) `chartOverIso`:
41	
42	* `exists_trivializing_cover` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): repackage the pointwise
43	  `IsLocallyTrivial` existential as an indexed affine cover.
44	* `chart_free_rank_one` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): the chart-local trivialisation.
45	* `chartPresentation` — built as `unitPresentation.ofIsIso chartOverIso.inv`; its
46	  `IsFinite` rides on the `ofIsIso` instance. Reduces to `chartOverIso`.
47	* `isFinitePresentation` — CLOSED modulo `chartOverIso`: assembles the
48	  `QuasicoherentData` from the cover + `chartPresentation`, `shrink`s the index
49	  into the site-object universe, and feeds `IsFinitePresentation.mk`.
50	* `isFiniteType` — CLOSED modulo `chartOverIso`: the Mathlib
51	  `IsFinitePresentation → IsFiniteType` instance.
52	
53	Two reusable axiom-clean bricks were added: `freeUnitIso` (`free PUnit ≅ unit R`)
54	and `unitPresentation` (the canonical finite presentation of the unit module,
55	one generator / no relations), with a `Presentation.IsFinite` instance.
56	
57	**`chartOverIso` (now redirected, no local `sorry`)** — is the over↔restrict
58	trivialisation bridge: an isomorphism `M.over U ≅ unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
59	transporting the given scheme-level trivialisation
60	`e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ unit (↑U).ringCatSheaf`. The two source categories are
61	equivalent via the open-immersion site equivalence
62	`Opens.overEquivalence U : Over U ≌ Opens ↥U` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over`);
63	lifting that equivalence to the level of *sheaves of modules* is the shared root
64	`Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence` of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`,
65	and the bridge is the general `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` built there. This file
66	now delegates to that construction (the same wall the dual lane consumes). See
67	`informal/chartOverIso.md`.
68	
69	The 5 pinned declarations are:
70	
71	1. `IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover` (`lem:lbc_trivializing_cover`) —
72	   repackage the pointwise existential of `IsLocallyTrivial` into an indexed
73	   trivialising affine cover.
74	2. `IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation` (`lem:lbc_chart_presentation`) — the
75	   trivial finite free presentation of `M.over U` on a trivialising chart.
76	3. `IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation` (`thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation`) —
77	   the main theorem: assemble a `QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising cover
78	   and feed it to `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`.
79	4. `IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType` (`cor:lbc_isFiniteType`) — finite type +
80	   quasi-coherence, immediate from finite presentation.
81	5. `IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one` (`lem:lbc_rank_flat`) — the chart-local
82	   rank-one free record.
83	
84	## Site instances (the chief de-risk; see `rem:lbc_site_instances`)
85	
86	Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation` / `QuasicoherentData` are stated
87	under three site hypotheses on the slice topologies `J.over U` of the underlying
88	topology `J = Opens.grothendieckTopology X` of `X.ringCatSheaf`:
89	
90	```
91	[∀ U, (J.over U).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
92	[∀ U, HasWeakSheafify (J.over U) AddCommGrpCat]     -- (resp. HasSheafify)
93	[∀ U, (J.over U).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
94	```
95	
96	The de-risk outcome (whether these resolve for `X.ringCatSheaf`) is recorded in
97	the task result and probed by the `#check` commands at the end of this file.
98	
99	## References
100	
101	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_LineBundleCoherence.tex`.
102	Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
103	(Tag 01CS, invertible modules), "Modules of finite presentation", and
104	Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M).
105	-/
106	
107	set_option autoImplicit false
108	
109	universe u
110	
111	open CategoryTheory Limits
112	
113	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
114	
115	namespace Scheme
116	
117	namespace LineBundle
118	
119	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
120	
121	/-! ## §1. From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover -/
122	
123	/-- **Trivialising affine cover** (Stacks 01CS / Definition 17.25.1).
124	A locally trivial module `M` admits an indexed trivialising affine cover: an
125	index type `I`, a family of affine opens `U i` covering `X`, and for each `i` a
126	`𝒪_{U_i}`-module isomorphism `M|_{U_i} ≅ 𝒪_{U_i}`.
127	
128	The witness repackages the pointwise existential `IsLocallyTrivial M` (take
129	`I := X` and assign each point its chosen neighbourhood). -/
130	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover {M : X.Modules}
131	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
132	    ∃ (I : Type u) (U : I → X.Opens),
133	      (∀ i, IsAffineOpen (U i)) ∧ iSup U = ⊤ ∧
134	        ∀ i, Nonempty (M.restrict (U i).ι ≅
135	          SheafOfModules.unit (U i : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
136	  refine ⟨X, fun x => (hM x).choose, fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.1, ?_,
137	    fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.2⟩
138	  rw [eq_top_iff]
139	  intro x _
140	  rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup]
141	  exact ⟨x, (hM x).choose_spec.1⟩
142	
143	/-! ## §1b. The canonical finite presentation of the unit module -/
144	
145	section UnitPresentation
146	
147	universe u₁ v₁
148	
149	variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
150	  {R : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}}
151	  [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{u}] [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat.{u}]
152	  [J.HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat.{u} AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
153	
154	/-- The canonical isomorphism `free PUnit ≅ unit R`: the free module on a single
155	generator is the unit module. -/
156	noncomputable def freeUnitIso :
157	    SheafOfModules.free.{u} (R := R) PUnit.{u + 1} ≅ SheafOfModules.unit R :=
158	  Limits.coproductUniqueIso (fun (_ : PUnit.{u + 1}) ↦ SheafOfModules.unit R)
159	
160	/-- The canonical generating sections of `unit R`: one generator (the image of
161	the free generator under `freeUnitIso`). -/
162	noncomputable def unitGenerators : (SheafOfModules.unit R).GeneratingSections where
163	  I := PUnit.{u + 1}
164	  s := (SheafOfModules.unit R).freeHomEquiv freeUnitIso.hom
165	  epi := by
166	    rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
167	    infer_instance
168	
169	/-- The canonical finite presentation of the unit module `unit R`: one generator,
170	no relations (its generating map is an isomorphism, so the relation kernel is the
171	zero object). -/
172	noncomputable def unitPresentation : (SheafOfModules.unit R).Presentation where
173	  generators := unitGenerators
174	  relations :=
175	    { I := PEmpty.{u + 1}
176	      s := PEmpty.elim
177	      epi := by
178	        have hiso : IsIso (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := by
179	          dsimp only [unitGenerators, SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.π]
180	          rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
181	          infer_instance
182	        have : Mono (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := inferInstance
183	        have hz : Limits.IsZero (Limits.kernel (unitGenerators (R := R)).π) :=
184	          Limits.isZero_kernel_of_mono _
185	        exact ⟨fun g h _ => hz.eq_of_src g h⟩ }
186	
187	instance : (unitPresentation (R := R)).IsFinite where
188	  isFiniteType_generators := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PUnit.{u + 1})⟩
189	  isFiniteType_relations := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PEmpty.{u + 1})⟩
190	
191	end UnitPresentation
192	
193	/-! ## §2. The trivial presentation on a chart -/
194	
195	/-- **The over↔restrict trivialisation bridge.** Transports the scheme-level
196	trivialisation `e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U` (an isomorphism in
197	`SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf`, the category of modules on the *open
198	subscheme* `U`) to a slice-level trivialisation
199	`M.over U ≅ 𝒪_U` (in `SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`, the category of
200	modules on the *slice site* `J.over U`).
201	
202	The two source categories are equivalent — the open-immersion functor
203	`U.ι.opensFunctor : Opens ↥U ≌ {V ∈ Opens X | V ≤ U} = Over U` induces an
204	equivalence `SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf ≌ SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
205	identifying `M.restrict U.ι ↦ M.over U` and `unit ↦ unit`. That modules-level
206	lift of `Opens.overEquivalence` is the shared root `Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence`
207	of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`, and the bridge is the general
208	`Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` built there as the three-step composite
209	`(restrictOverIso).symm ≪≫ overEquivalence.functor.mapIso e ≪≫ unitOverIso`.
210	
211	This local declaration now **redirects** to that general construction (the
212	iter-258 shared-root pivot), so it carries no `sorry` of its own; the remaining
213	mathematical content (the three isos `overEquivalence` / `restrictOverIso` /
214	`unitOverIso`) lives in the shared-root file where the dual lane consumes it too.
215	Everything else in this engine (`chartPresentation`, `isFinitePresentation`,
216	`isFiniteType`) was already complete modulo this isomorphism. -/
217	noncomputable def chartOverIso (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
218	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
219	    M.over U ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U) :=
220	  Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso U M e
221	
222	/-- **Finite free presentation on a chart** (Stacks, "Modules of finite
223	presentation"). On a trivialising chart `U` — i.e. with an isomorphism
224	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` — the restriction `M.over U` admits the trivial finite free
225	presentation (one generator, no relations), obtained by transporting the
226	canonical presentation of `SheafOfModules.unit` along the trivialisation
227	(through the `chartOverIso` bridge). -/
228	noncomputable def IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
229	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
230	    (M.over U).Presentation := by
231	  exact SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U)
232	    (M := SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)) (N := SheafOfModules.over M U)
233	    (chartOverIso M U e).inv (unitPresentation (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U))
234	
235	instance (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
236	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
237	    (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M U e).IsFinite := by
238	  dsimp only [IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation]
239	  infer_instance
240	
241	/-! ## §3. Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle -/
242	
243	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finitely presented** (Stacks 0B8M / Lemma 17.25.4).
244	A locally trivial module on a scheme is finitely presented: assemble a
245	`QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising affine cover
246	(`exists_trivializing_cover`), whose presentation at each index is the finite
247	free presentation of `chartPresentation`, and feed it to
248	`SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`. No stalk computation or
249	neighbourhood-spreading is involved. -/
250	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation {M : X.Modules}
251	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
252	    M.IsFinitePresentation := by
253	  obtain ⟨I, U, hUaff, hUtop, hUiso⟩ := hM.exists_trivializing_cover
254	  -- assemble the quasi-coherent data, one finite chart presentation per index;
255	  -- `shrink` lands the index type in the site-object universe demanded by
256	  -- `IsFinitePresentation`'s existential.
257	  let q : M.QuasicoherentData :=
258	    { I := I
259	      X := U
260	      coversTop := by
261	        -- the trivialising affine opens cover the terminal object of the site
262	        intro W x hxW
263	        have hx : x ∈ iSup U := by rw [hUtop]; trivial
264	        rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup] at hx
265	        obtain ⟨i, hi⟩ := hx
266	        exact ⟨U i ⊓ W, homOfLE inf_le_right, ⟨i, ⟨homOfLE inf_le_left⟩⟩, hi, hxW⟩
267	      presentation := fun i => IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M (U i) (hUiso i).some }
268	  have hsh : q.shrink.IsFinitePresentation := by
269	    apply SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation.mk
270	    intro i
271	    exact inferInstanceAs (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M _ _).IsFinite
272	  exact { exists_quasicoherentData := ⟨q.shrink, hsh⟩ }
273	
274	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finite type and quasi-coherent** (corollary of
275	`isFinitePresentation`). Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
276	`SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation` and quasi-coherence by
277	`SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation`. -/
278	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType {M : X.Modules}
279	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
280	    M.IsFiniteType := by
281	  haveI := hM.isFinitePresentation
282	  infer_instance
283	
284	/-! ## §4. Rank one and flatness record -/
285	
286	/-- **Chart-local rank one and flatness** (Stacks, "Locally free sheaves":
287	finite locally free of rank `r`). On a trivialising chart the restriction
288	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` is free of rank one over `𝒪_U`; in particular flat (`𝒪_U` is a flat
289	module over itself, and flatness is preserved by the isomorphism). The fact is
290	recorded chart-locally — as the rank-one free model `M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U = ⊕_{*} 𝒪_U`
291	the Quot embedding consumes — because Mathlib has no `SheafOfModules`-level
292	locally-free / flat / rank predicate. -/
293	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one {M : X.Modules}
294	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) (x : X) :
295	    ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
296	      Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
297	  exact hM x
298	
299	end LineBundle
300	
301	end Scheme
302	
303	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}
98	
99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  \leanok
136	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
137	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
138	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
139	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
140	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
141	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
142	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
143	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
147	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
148	
149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
150	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
151	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
153	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
154	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
155	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
156	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
159	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
160	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
161	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
162	  %  $$
163	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
164	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
165	  %  \longrightarrow
166	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
167	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
168	  %  $$"
169	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
170	  presentation''.}
171	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
172	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
173	  trivial finite free presentation
174	  \[
175	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
176	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
177	  \]
178	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
179	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
180	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
181	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
182	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
183	  this presentation is finite.
184	\end{lemma}
185	
186	\begin{proof}
187	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
188	  \leanok
189	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
190	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
191	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
192	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
193	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
194	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
195	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
196	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
197	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
198	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
199	
200	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
201	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
202	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
203	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
204	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
205	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
206	  % NOTE. The over-restrict categorical bridge is the shared construction
207	  % `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` (lem:chart_over_iso,
208	  % chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence).
209	\end{proof}
210	
211	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
212	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
213	
214	\begin{theorem}\leanok
215	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
216	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
218	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
219	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
220	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
221	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
224	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
225	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
226	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
227	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
228	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
229	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
230	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
231	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
232	\end{theorem}
233	
234	\begin{proof}
235	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
236	  \leanok
237	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
238	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
239	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
240	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
241	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
242	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
243	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
244	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
245	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
246	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
247	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
248	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
249	
250	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
251	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
252	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
253	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
254	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{corollary}\leanok
258	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
259	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
260	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
261	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
262	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
263	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). Quasi-coherence
264	  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows from finite presentation via
265	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
266	\end{corollary}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
270	  \leanok
271	  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
272	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), immediate
273	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}. Quasi-coherence is immediate from
274	  finite presentation by
275	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\section{Rank one and flatness record}
279	\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}
280	
281	% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
282	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
283	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
284	% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
285	% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
286	% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.
287	
288	\begin{lemma}\leanok
289	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
290	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
292	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
293	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
294	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
295	  % locally free of rank $r$")
296	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
298	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
299	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
300	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
301	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
302	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
303	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
304	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
305	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
306	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
307	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
308	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
309	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
310	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
311	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
312	  rank-one free \emph{model}: rank-one freeness and flatness are
313	  \emph{consequences} of this iso, because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
314	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
315	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the sense of
316	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite locally free
317	  of rank one and flat on the cover.
318	\end{lemma}
319	
320	\begin{proof}
321	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
322	  \leanok
323	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
324	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
325	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
326	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
327	  rank is one. The properties are recorded chart-locally because rank-one
328	  freeness and flatness are local conditions, and the chart-local data are what
329	  the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial input
330	  on a fibre.
331	\end{proof}

You are a prover agent for project 'Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge'. Current stage: prover.
Archon iteration: 265.
Project directory: /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge
Project state directory: /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon
Read /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/CLAUDE.md for your role, then read /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/PROGRESS.md.
Check your .lean file for /- USER: ... -/ comments for file-specific hints.

IMPORTANT:
- You own ONLY the file assigned below. Do NOT edit any other .lean file.
- Write your results to /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md when done.
- Do NOT edit PROGRESS.md, task_pending.md, or task_done.md.
- Missing Mathlib infrastructure is NEVER a valid reason to leave a sorry.
- NEVER revert to a bare sorry. Always leave your partial proof attempt in the code.

Blueprint chapter for your file: blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex
- Read it BEFORE writing any Lean code — it contains the informal proof
  written by the plan agent.
- After you formalize a declaration, mark its blueprint environment with \leanok.
- If the chapter file does not yet exist, create it with a minimal \chapter block
  and note in your task_results that the plan agent should flesh it out.
Active prover mode: **fine-grained**

## Your goal

Translate each sentence of the informal proof in the blueprint chapter into an atomic named Lean lemma, then prove each lemma independently. The theorem is never attacked whole. Progress is measured per-sentence, not per-theorem. If sentence themselves can be decomposed further, do so. 

## Why this matters

Provers stall on large goals because the first difficult step blocks the rest. By forcing each sentence to become its own named claim, every step either closes quickly or fails with a precise, fixable error — instead of a vague "I can't prove this big thing."

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. **Read your blueprint chapter sentence by sentence.** For the section corresponding to your assigned theorem, list every individual mathematical claim made in the informal proof (not just top-level lemmas — every "since X", "by Y", "note that Z" is a candidate).
3. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts and dead ends.
4. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments.
5. For each identified sentence:
   a. Introduce a named `private lemma` encoding that sentence's claim.
   b. Immediately attempt to prove it — using LSP tools, Mathlib search, and tactic exploration.
   c. If it closes: mark RESOLVED in your log; move on.
   d. If it does not close within reasonable effort: leave a `sorry` with the partial tactic attempt visible (never revert to bare `sorry`), naming the specific blocker; move on to the next sentence.
6. After all sentences are attempted, assemble the main theorem from the sub-lemma names (the assembly itself may use `sorry` if some sub-lemmas are still open).
7. Verify the file compiles.
8. Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, other agents' files, or the blueprint chapter.

## Sentence identification heuristics

Read the informal proof text and extract each **atomic mathematical claim** — a sentence that, if true, advances the proof by one logical step. Examples:

- "The map φ is injective" → `lemma phi_injective : Function.Injective φ := …`
- "Since R is local, the maximal ideal is unique" → `lemma unique_maximal_of_local [IsLocalRing R] : … := …`
- "The kernel is contained in the Jacobson radical" → `lemma ker_le_jacobson : … := …`

Skip claims that are definitional (follow immediately from `unfold` or `simp`) — fold those into the first proof that needs them.

## Prove each sentence atomically

For each sub-lemma:

1. State the type precisely — it must exactly encode the blueprint sentence.
2. Try `simp`, `ring`, `omega`, `norm_num`, `exact?`, `apply?` as quick checks.
3. Use `lean_leansearch` with the mathematical content as the query.
4. If a Mathlib lemma covers it exactly: `exact MathLibLemmaName` or `apply`.
5. If it requires a short tactic proof: write it.
6. If it requires significant work: write whatever partial proof you have, then leave a scoped `sorry` at the stuck point. Do NOT revert to a bare `sorry` — keep the partial attempt visible.

**You are fully authorized to make every proof decision within your assigned file without waiting for the planner.** "I'll let the planner decide" is never valid for a tactic or approach choice — make the call, attempt it, document the outcome.

**Time budget**: spend at most a few tool calls per sentence before declaring PARTIAL and moving on. The point of this mode is coverage across all sentences, not depth on any single one.

**Comments are not progress. Code is.** If you write `-- TODO: try simp [X]` or `/- This could work via Y -/`, you must attempt it before writing the comment. Only leave a comment about an approach if you tried it and it failed with a named error.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. Do not change protected signatures.

## Completion criteria

This mode succeeds when:

- Every informal sentence has a corresponding named sub-lemma (proved or `sorry`).
- The main theorem's proof body references the sub-lemma names.
- The file compiles.

A subsequent `prove` pass targeting specific open sub-lemmas is expected.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). Never call them as shell commands.

- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file.
- Use `lean_goal` after each tactic to confirm progress.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` — describe the mathematical content of the sentence.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Logging

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff — fine-grained pass

### Sentence 1: "The map φ is injective" → `phi_injective`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — `exact PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective`

### Sentence 2: "The kernel is contained in the Jacobson radical" → `ker_le_jacobson`
- **Result:** PARTIAL — started from `Ideal.mem_jacobson_iff`, stuck at membership criterion
- **Dead end:** `exact?` found nothing; `Ideal.jacobson_mono` applies but needs surjectivity first
- **Next step:** prove surjectivity (sentence 4) first, then return here

### Sentence 3: "Since R is local, the maximal ideal is unique" → `unique_maximal_of_local`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — direct from `IsLocalRing.eq_maximalIdeal`

### Assembly (wLocal_iff)
- References: `phi_injective`, `ker_le_jacobson` (sorry), `unique_maximal_of_local`
- Compiles with scoped sorry on `ker_le_jacobson`.

## Summary
- 2/3 sentences closed; 1 blocked (kernel ⊆ Jacobson).
- Recommended next: `prove` targeting `ker_le_jacobson` with directive to establish surjectivity first.
```

## End-of-session handoff

**Before declaring done, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment or TODO? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. After all sentences, did I try to fully assemble the main theorem? Even with sorry sub-lemmas, a structured assembly often reveals which sub-lemmas are actually easy.
3. Are there open sub-lemmas I judged "too hard" without a genuine attempt? Spend one more round on each.

Before writing results:

1. Verify the file compiles.
2. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with one entry per identified sentence: name, type (brief), result (RESOLVED / PARTIAL / sorry with blocker).
3. **Write a `## Summary` section**: N/M sentences closed, sorry count before → after, which sentences are open and exactly why.
4. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be honest; only closed sub-lemma proofs count as progress. Sub-lemmas extracted with only `sorry` bodies and no proof attempt are cosmetics:
   - `Real progress`: M/N sentences closed — list which ones and sorry count before → after.
   - `Partial progress`: measurable code advance (partial proof bodies, one branch closed); list open sentences with their specific blockers (not "it's hard" — the exact type mismatch or missing ingredient).
   - `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt — name each. Planner will re-assign with directive to attempt them.
   - `Blueprint sentences not identifiable`: the informal proof is too vague to extract atomic sentences — describe what's missing and what the blueprint needs.
   - `All sentences already proved`: file had no sorry to decompose.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain the deviation.

content: I'll start by reading my role, progress, and the blueprint chapter for my assigned file.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.

2699	  pullbackTensorMap h (f^*M) (f^*N) ≫ tensorObjIsoOfIso (pullbackComp h f) (pullbackComp h f)`.

2701	The base-change-square form of the blueprint (`f ∘ j' = j ∘ g` with `j, j'` open immersions) is the
2702	specialisation `h := j'`, `f`, applied to the two factorisations `j' ≫ f = g ≫ j` of the equal

2711	lemma pullbackTensorMap_restrict {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (h : Z ⟶ Y) (f : Y ⟶ X)

2714	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp h f).inv.app (tensorObj M N) ≫
2715	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map (pullbackTensorMap f M N) ≫
2716	      pullbackTensorMap h ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj M)
2717	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj N) ≫
2718	      (tensorObjIsoOfIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp h f).app M)
2719	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp h f).app N)).hom := by

2723	  --   S2 = a_·.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ'_{·}) M.val N.val),
2724	  --   S3 = (sheafifyTensorUnitIso (Fp M.val) (Fp N.val)).hom,
2725	  --   S4 = a_·.map (forget(pullbackValIso · M).hom ⊗ₘ forget(pullbackValIso · N).hom).
2726	  -- Unlike D1′ (naturality, a 4-square *paste*), this is a 4-square *composition*-coherence: the LHS
2727	  -- is the composite-morphism `· = h ≫ f` instance, the RHS interleaves the `f` instance (pushed

/-!
# Pushforward of sheaves of modules

Assume that categories `C` and `D` are equipped with Grothendieck topologies, and
that `F : C ⥤ D` is a continuous functor.
Then, if `φ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R` is
a morphism of sheaves of rings, we construct the pushforward functor
`pushforward φ : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ SheafOfModules.{v} S`, and
we show that they interact with the composition of morphisms similarly as pseudofunctors.

-/

@[expose] public section

universe w v' u' v v₁ v₂ v₃ v₄ u₁ u₂ u₃ u₄ u

open CategoryTheory Functor

namespace SheafOfModules

variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {D : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂} D]
  {D' : Type u₃} [Category.{v₃} D'] {D'' : Type u₄} [Category.{v₄} D'']
  {J : GrothendieckTopology C} {K : GrothendieckTopology D} {F : C ⥤ D}
  {S : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}} {R : Sheaf K RingCat.{u}}
  [Functor.IsContinuous F J K]
  (φ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R)

/-- The pushforward of sheaves of modules that is induced by a continuous functor `F`
and a morphism of sheaves of rings `φ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat J K).obj R`. -/
@[simps map_val, simps -isSimp obj_val]
noncomputable def pushforward : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ SheafOfModules.{v} S where
  obj M :=
    { val := (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ.hom).obj M.val
      isSheaf := ((F.sheafPushforwardContinuous _ J K).obj ⟨_, M.isSheaf⟩).property }
  map f :=
    { val := (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ.hom).map f.val }

/-- Given `M : SheafOfModules R` and `X : D`, this is the restriction of `M`
over the sheaf of rings `R.over X` on the category `Over X`. -/
noncomputable abbrev over (M : SheafOfModules.{v} R) (X : D) : SheafOfModules.{v} (R.over X) :=
  (pushforward.{v} (𝟙 _)).obj M

/-- Given a map `f : M ⟶ N` between sheaves of modules over `R`, this is the restriction
to the map `M.over X ⟶ N.over X` between sheaves of modules over `R.over X`. -/
noncomputable abbrev Hom.over {M N : SheafOfModules.{v} R} (f : M ⟶ N) (X : D) :
    M.over X ⟶ N.over X :=
  (pushforward.{v} (𝟙 _)).map f

section

variable (R) in
/-- The pushforward functor by the identity morphism identifies to
the identify functor of the category of sheaves of modules. -/
noncomputable def pushforwardId :
    pushforward.{v} (S := R) (F := 𝟭 _) (𝟙 R) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
  Iso.refl _

/-- Pushforwards along equal morphisms of sheaves of rings are isomorphic. -/
noncomputable
def pushforwardCongr {φ ψ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R} (e : φ = ψ) :
    pushforward.{v} φ ≅ pushforward.{v} ψ :=
  NatIso.ofComponents (fun X ↦ (SheafOfModules.fullyFaithfulForget _).preimageIso
    (PresheafOfModules.isoMk (fun U ↦ (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsCongr (by subst e; rfl)).app _)
      fun _ _ _ ↦ by subst e; rfl)) fun _ ↦ by subst e; rfl

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_symm
    {φ ψ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R} (e : φ = ψ) :
  (pushforwardCongr e).symm = pushforwardCongr e.symm := rfl

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_hom_app_val_app
    {φ ψ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R} (e : φ = ψ) (M U x) :
  ((pushforwardCongr e).hom.app M).val.app U x = x := rfl

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_inv_app_val_app
    {φ ψ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R} (e : φ = ψ) (M U x) :
  ((pushforwardCongr e).inv.app M).val.app U x = x := rfl

section

variable {K' : GrothendieckTopology D'} {K'' : GrothendieckTopology D''}
  {G : D ⥤ D'} {R' : Sheaf K' RingCat.{u}}
  [Functor.IsContinuous G K K'] [Functor.IsContinuous (F ⋙ G) J K']
  (ψ : R ⟶ (G.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} K K').obj R')

#adaptation_note /-- After nightly-2026-02-23 we need this to avoid timeouts. -/
/-- The composition of two pushforward functors on categories of sheaves of modules
identify to the pushforward for the composition. -/
noncomputable def pushforwardComp :
    pushforward.{v} ψ ⋙ pushforward.{v} φ ≅
      pushforward.{v} (F := F ⋙ G) (φ ≫ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).map ψ) :=
  Iso.refl _

-- Not a simp because the type of the LHS is dsimp-able
lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_val_app (M U x) :
  ((pushforwardComp φ ψ).hom.app M).val.app U x = x := rfl

-- Not a simp because the type of the LHS is dsimp-able
lemma pushforwardComp_inv_app_val_app (M U x) :
  ((pushforwardComp φ ψ).inv.app M).val.app U x = x := rfl

variable {G' : D' ⥤ D''} {R'' : Sheaf K'' RingCat.{u}}
  [Functor.IsContinuous G' K' K'']
  [Functor.IsContinuous (G ⋙ G') K K'']
  [Functor.IsContinuous ((F ⋙ G) ⋙ G') J K'']
  [Functor.IsContinuous (F ⋙ G ⋙ G') J K'']
  (ψ' : R' ⟶ (G'.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} K' K'').obj R'')

lemma pushforward_assoc :
    (pushforward ψ').isoWhiskerLeft (pushforwardComp φ ψ) ≪≫
      pushforwardComp (F := F ⋙ G)
        (φ ≫ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).map ψ) ψ' =
    ((pushforward ψ').associator (pushforward ψ) (pushforward φ)).symm ≪≫
      isoWhiskerRight (pushforwardComp ψ ψ') (pushforward φ) ≪≫
      pushforwardComp (G := G ⋙ G') φ (ψ ≫
        (G.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} K K').map ψ') := by ext; rfl

end

lemma pushforward_comp_id :
    pushforwardComp.{v} (F := 𝟭 C) (𝟙 S) φ =
      isoWhiskerLeft (pushforward.{v} φ) (pushforwardId S) ≪≫ rightUnitor _ := by ext; rfl

lemma pushforward_id_comp :
    pushforwardComp.{v} (G := 𝟭 _) φ (𝟙 R) =
      isoWhiskerRight (pushforwardId R) (pushforward.{v} φ) ≪≫ leftUnitor _ := by ext; rfl

end

section NatTrans

variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {D : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂} D]
  {J : GrothendieckTopology C} {K : GrothendieckTopology D}
  {F G H : C ⥤ D} {T : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}} {S : Sheaf K RingCat.{u}}
  [Functor.IsContinuous F J K]
  [Functor.IsContinuous G J K]
  [Functor.IsContinuous H J K]
  (φ : T ⟶ (G.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj S)

/-- A natural transformation gives a natural transformation between the pushforward functors. -/
noncomputable
def pushforwardNatTrans (α : F ⟶ G) :
    pushforward.{v} φ ⟶
      pushforward.{v} (φ ≫ (Functor.sheafPushforwardContinuousNatTrans α _ _ _).app S) where
  app X :=
  { val.app U := (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (φ.hom.app U).hom).map (X.val.map (α.app U.unop).op)
    val.naturality {U V} i := by
      ext x
      dsimp
      change (X.val.presheaf.map (G.map i.unop).op ≫ X.val.presheaf.map (α.app V.unop).op) _ =
        (X.val.presheaf.map (α.app U.unop).op ≫ X.val.presheaf.map (F.map i.unop).op) _
      simp only [← CategoryTheory.Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp, α.naturality] }
  naturality {X Y} f := by
    ext U x
    exact congr($(f.val.naturality (α.app U.unop).op) x).symm

@[simp] lemma pushforwardNatTrans_app_val_app (α : F ⟶ G) (M U x) :
    ((pushforwardNatTrans φ α).app M).val.app U x = M.val.map (α.app U.unop).op x := rfl

@[simp]
lemma pushforwardNatTrans_id :
    pushforwardNatTrans φ (𝟙 G) = (pushforwardCongr (by cat_disch)).hom := by cat_disch

@[simp]
lemma pushforwardNatTrans_comp (α : F ⟶ G) (β : G ⟶ H)
    (φ : T ⟶ (H.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj S) :
    pushforwardNatTrans φ (α ≫ β) = pushforwardNatTrans φ β ≫ pushforwardNatTrans _ α ≫
      (pushforwardCongr (by cat_disch)).hom := by cat_disch

@[simp]
lemma pushforwardNatTrans_app_val_app_apply (α : F ⟶ G) (X U x) :
    ((pushforwardNatTrans φ α).app X).val.app U x = X.val.map (α.app U.unop).op x := rfl

/-- A natural isomorphism gives a natural isomorphism between the pushforward functors. -/
@[simps]
noncomputable def pushforwardNatIso (α : F ≅ G) :
    pushforward.{v} φ ≅
      pushforward.{v} (φ ≫ (Functor.sheafPushforwardContinuousNatTrans α.hom _ _ _).app S) where
  hom := pushforwardNatTrans _ α.hom
  inv := pushforwardNatTrans _ α.inv ≫
    (pushforwardCongr (by ext : 3; simp [← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp])).hom
  hom_inv_id := by
    ext X U x
    suffices X.val.presheaf.map (α.hom.app U.unop).op ≫
      X.val.presheaf.map (α.inv.app U.unop).op = 𝟙 _ from congr($this x)
    simp only [← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp,
      Iso.inv_hom_id_app, op_id, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]
  inv_hom_id := by
    ext X U x
    suffices X.val.presheaf.map (α.inv.app U.unop).op ≫
      X.val.presheaf.map (α.hom.app U.unop).op = 𝟙 _ from congr($this x)
    simp only [← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp,
      Iso.hom_inv_id_app, op_id, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]

end NatTrans

section Adjunction

variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {D : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂} D]
  {J : GrothendieckTopology C} {K : GrothendieckTopology D} {F : C ⥤ D} {G : D ⥤ C}
  {S : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}} {R : Sheaf K RingCat.{u}}
  [Functor.IsContinuous F J K]
  [Functor.IsContinuous G K J]
  (adj : F ⊣ G)
  (φ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R)
  (ψ : R ⟶ (G.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} K J).obj S)
  (H₁ : Functor.whiskerRight (NatTrans.op adj.counit) R.obj = ψ.hom ≫ G.op.whiskerLeft φ.hom)
  (H₂ : φ.hom ≫ F.op.whiskerLeft ψ.hom ≫
    Functor.whiskerRight (NatTrans.op adj.unit) S.obj = 𝟙 S.obj)

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/-- If `F ⊣ G`, then the pushforwards along `F` and `G` are also adjoint. -/
noncomputable
def pushforwardPushforwardAdj : pushforward.{v} φ ⊣ pushforward.{v} ψ where
  unit :=
    letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp G F K J K
    (pushforwardId _).inv ≫ pushforwardNatTrans (𝟙 _) adj.counit ≫
      (pushforwardCongr (by ext1; simpa)).hom ≫ (pushforwardComp _ _).inv
  counit :=
    letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp F G J K J
    (pushforwardComp _ _).hom ≫ pushforwardNatTrans _ adj.unit ≫
      (pushforwardCongr (by ext1; simpa)).hom ≫ (pushforwardId _).hom
  left_triangle_components X := by
    ext U x
    change (X.val.presheaf.map (adj.counit.app (F.obj U.unop)).op ≫
      X.val.presheaf.map (F.map (adj.unit.app U.unop)).op) _ = _
    dsimp only [id_obj]
    rw [← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp, adj.left_triangle_components]
    simp
  right_triangle_components X := by
    ext U x
    change (X.val.presheaf.map (G.map (adj.counit.app U.unop)).op ≫
      X.val.presheaf.map (adj.unit.app (G.obj U.unop)).op) _ = _
    rw [← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp, adj.right_triangle_components]
    simp

-- Not a simp because the type of the LHS is dsimp-able
lemma pushforwardPushforwardAdj_unit_app_val_app (M U x) :
    ((pushforwardPushforwardAdj adj φ ψ H₁ H₂).unit.app M).val.app U x =
      M.val.map (adj.counit.app U.unop).op x := rfl

-- Not a simp because the type of the LHS is dsimp-able
lemma pushforwardPushforwardAdj_counit_app_val_app (M U x) :
    ((pushforwardPushforwardAdj adj φ ψ H₁ H₂).counit.app M).val.app U x =
      M.val.map (adj.unit.app U.unop).op x := rfl

noncomputable section

open CategoryTheory Limits

variable {C : Type u'} [Category.{v'} C] [HasBinaryProducts C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
  {R : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}}

/-- The canonical morphism from `R` to the pushforward of its restriction to `Over x`. -/
def pushforwardOver (x : C) :
    R ⟶ ((Over.star x).sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat J (J.over x)).obj (R.over x) :=
  ⟨{app U := R.obj.map Limits.prod.snd.op
    naturality U V f := by simp [← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp]; rfl }⟩

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/-- The adjunction between restriction to `Over x` and pushforward along `Over.star x`. -/
def overPushforwardOverAdj (x : C) :
    pushforward.{w} (𝟙 (R.over x)) ⊣ pushforward.{w} (pushforwardOver x) := by
  refine pushforwardPushforwardAdj (Over.forgetAdjStar x) (𝟙 (R.over x)) _ ?_ ?_
  · ext y : 2
    simp [pushforwardOver]
  · ext y : 2
    simp [pushforwardOver, ← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp]

instance (x : C) : IsLeftAdjoint (pushforward.{w} (𝟙 (R.over x))) where
  exists_rightAdjoint := ⟨_, Nonempty.intro (overPushforwardOverAdj x)⟩

end

end Adjunction

section Equivalence

variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {D : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂} D]
  {J : GrothendieckTopology C} {K : GrothendieckTopology D} (eqv : C ≌ D)
  {S : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}} {R : Sheaf K RingCat.{u}}
  [Functor.IsContinuous eqv.functor J K]
  [Functor.IsContinuous eqv.inverse K J]
  (φ : S ⟶ (eqv.functor.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R)
  (ψ : R ⟶ (eqv.inverse.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} K J).obj S)
  (H₁ : Functor.whiskerRight (NatTrans.op eqv.counit) R.obj =
    ψ.hom ≫ eqv.inverse.op.whiskerLeft φ.hom)
  (H₂ : φ.hom ≫ eqv.functor.op.whiskerLeft ψ.hom ≫
    Functor.whiskerRight (NatTrans.op eqv.unit) S.obj = 𝟙 S.obj)

/-- If `e : C ≌ D`, then the pushforwards along `e.functor` and `e.inverse` forms an equivalence. -/
noncomputable
def pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence : SheafOfModules R ≌ SheafOfModules S where
  functor := pushforward.{v} φ
  inverse := pushforward.{v} ψ
  unitIso :=
    letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp eqv.inverse eqv.functor K J K
    (pushforwardId _).symm ≪≫ pushforwardNatIso _ eqv.counitIso ≪≫
      pushforwardCongr (by ext1; simpa) ≪≫ (pushforwardComp _ _).symm
  counitIso :=
    letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp eqv.functor eqv.inverse J K J
    pushforwardComp _ _ ≪≫ pushforwardNatIso _ eqv.unitIso ≪≫
      pushforwardCongr (by ext1; simpa) ≪≫ pushforwardId _
  functor_unitIso_comp :=
    (pushforwardPushforwardAdj eqv.toAdjunction φ ψ H₁ H₂).left_triangle_components

-- Not a simp because the type of the LHS is dsimp-able
lemma pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_unit_app_val_app (M U x) :
    ((pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence eqv φ ψ H₁ H₂).unit.app M).val.app U x =
      M.val.map (eqv.counit.app U.unop).op x := rfl

-- Not a simp because the type of the LHS is dsimp-able
lemma pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_counit_app_val_app (M U x) :
    ((pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence eqv φ ψ H₁ H₂).counit.app M).val.app U x =
      M.val.map (eqv.unit.app U.unop).op x := rfl

end Equivalence

end SheafOfModules

/-- **STEP-1 bridge (presheaf↔sheaf pushforward compatibility, the binding obligation of the D3′
Sq1 tail).** The forgetful functor `SheafOfModules.forget` intertwines the sheaf-level
`SheafOfModules.pushforward φ` with the presheaf-level `PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ.hom`:
for any morphism `g` of sheaves of modules over `R`,
`forget.map ((pushforward φ).map g) = (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ.hom).map (forget.map g)`.

This is the compatibility named in the blueprint's `lem:pullback_tensor_map_basechange` Sq1-tail
binding-obligation paragraph: it is what lets the recovered sheaf-level `B_f`/`B_h` unit factors
(which live under `SheafOfModules.pushforward`) be slid across into the presheaf-level
`PresheafOfModules.pushforward` of the unit identity.  It is *definitional* — `SheafOfModules.pushforward`
is built sectionwise from `PresheafOfModules.pushforward` (`pushforward_map_val`) and `forget` is the
`.val` projection (`forget_map`), so the two sides are equal by `rfl`. -/
private lemma forget_map_pushforward_map
    {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {D : Type u} [Category.{u} D]
    {J : GrothendieckTopology C} {K : GrothendieckTopology D} {F : C ⥤ D}
    {S : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}} {R : Sheaf K RingCat.{u}} [Functor.IsContinuous F J K]
    (φ : S ⟶ (F.sheafPushforwardContinuous RingCat.{u} J K).obj R)
    {A B : SheafOfModules.{u} R} (g : A ⟶ B) :
    (SheafOfModules.forget S).map ((SheafOfModules.pushforward φ).map g) =
      (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ.hom).map ((SheafOfModules.forget R).map g) := by
  rfl

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean — iter-265 (fine-grained, D3′ Sq1 tail)

Objective 2: STEP 1 = extract the presheaf↔sheaf compatibility bridge as a named lemma and prove
it axiom-clean; STEP 2 = assemble `sheafificationCompPullback_comp_tail` (a)–(e).

## Sentence 1 — STEP-1 binding-obligation bridge → `forget_map_pushforward_map`
- **Type:** `(SheafOfModules.forget S).map ((SheafOfModules.pushforward φ).map g) =
  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ.hom).map ((SheafOfModules.forget R).map g)` for a general
  continuous-functor sheaf-ring morphism `φ` and a sheaf-of-modules morphism `g`.
- **Result:** **RESOLVED — axiom-clean (`rfl`).** Verified via `lean_verify`: axioms =
  `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`, **no `sorryAx`**.
- **Why it's `rfl`:** `SheafOfModules.pushforward` is defined sectionwise from
  `PresheafOfModules.pushforward` (`@[simps map_val]` ⇒ `pushforward_map_val`), and
  `SheafOfModules.forget` is the `.val` projection (`forget_map`); the two sides are definitionally
  equal. Exactly the planner's expectation ("likely near-definitional").
- This is the precise blocker the iter-264 prover named (the presheaf↔sheaf forget/pushforward
  compatibility bridge feeding the recovered units). **It is the sanctioned measurable decompose
  deliverable for this iteration.**

## Sentence 2 — wire the bridge into the tail (blueprint step (d)) → in-proof `erw`
- **Result:** **RESOLVED (structural).** `erw [forget_map_pushforward_map]` *fires* on the tail goal
  and rewrites the second RHS factor `forget.map ((SheafOfModules.pushforward φ_{h≫f}).map (R1≫R5≫δ))`
  to the presheaf-level `(PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ'_{h≫f}).map (forget.map (R1≫R5≫δ))`,
  crossing the sheaf↔presheaf boundary.
- **Finding:** plain `rw [forget_map_pushforward_map]` does NOT fire (defeq-not-syntactic
  `SheafOfModules.pushforward`/`forget` composite) — **`erw` is required**, confirming the bridge is
  genuinely the needed device, not cosmetic.

## Sentence 3 — split RHS first factor (blueprint step (e.0)) → in-proof `rw [Functor.map_comp]`
- **Result:** **RESOLVED (structural).** `rw [Functor.map_comp]` (PLAIN, not `erw`) splits
  `forget.map (B'_unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom)` into `forget.map B'_unit ≫ forget.map pushforwardComp.hom`
  without touching the LHS.
- **Finding (recorded in-file):** an `erw` here instead matches the LHS
  `pushforward.map (sheafAdj_Z.unit.app _)` by defeq and UNFOLDS the unit into its
  `toSheafify ≫ restrictHomEquivOfIsLocallySurjective` expansion — the exact contamination the
  iter-262 R0-peel comment warned about one square up. Plain `rw` is the safe choice.

## Sentence 4 — R1/R5 recovery + slide (blueprint steps (ii)–(iii)) → `sorry` (BLOCKED)
- **Result:** **PARTIAL / sorry with precise blocker.** Remaining goal documented in-file:
  `B_{h≫f}.unit P = a_X.unit P ≫ (forget.map B'_unit ≫ forget.map pushforwardComp.hom)
   ≫ pushforward φ'_{h≫f}.map (forget.map (R1 ≫ R5 ≫ δ_pre))`.
- **Exact blocker:** recovering R1/R5 as the composite-adjunction units `B_f.unit`/`B_h.unit` needs a
  `have` that reframes `forget.map ((pullback h).map (sheafCompPb f .app P).hom)` through the
  f-adjunction `homEquiv` (the twin of the model's `hinner` L952–973 in
  `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp`), so that `leftAdjointUniqUnitEta_app` (axiom-clean, L1668) can fire.
  The bare goal has no `homEquiv` head. The `unitToPushforwardObjUnit`-analog at the
  composite (sheafify∘pushforward) adjunction level is **absent** — in the model it was `rfl`
  (`unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp`), here the units are composite adjunctions so it is NOT `rfl`. This
  is the genuinely-novel sheafification-laden mate step.
- Next: `leftAdjointUniqUnitEta_app` recovery → slide `(SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp h f).hom` past
  via `.hom.naturality` → collapse `Adjunction.comp_unit_app` + `Adjunction.unit_naturality` to
  `B_{h≫f}.unit` (model L997–1001).

## Assembly (`sheafificationCompPullback_comp_tail`)
- Proof body now: `rw [restrictScalarsId_map]; conv_rhs => rw [Functor.map_comp]` (DONE iter-264) →
  `erw [forget_map_pushforward_map]` (step d, NEW) → `rw [Functor.map_comp]` (step e.0, NEW) → `sorry`.
- Compiles. Caller `sheafificationCompPullback_comp` unchanged (signature of tail unchanged).

## Summary
- **3/4 sentences closed** (the bridge axiom-clean + two structural wiring steps committed and
  compiling); 1 open (R1/R5 recovery).
- **Sorry count: 3 → 3** (file-level unchanged) BUT **+1 new axiom-clean named sub-lemma**
  (`forget_map_pushforward_map`) — the pc265-sanctioned measurable decompose deliverable. The tail
  `sheafificationCompPullback_comp_tail` advanced from `…conv_rhs; sorry` to
  `…conv_rhs; erw [bridge]; rw [map_comp]; sorry` (2 committed steps consumed).
- Other sorries untouched: L720 `exists_tensorObj_inverse` (gated), L~2858 `pullbackTensorMap_restrict`
  (gated). No protected signatures touched. File compiles (no errors), RACE-safe for DualInverse.lean.

## Why I stopped
- **Real progress:** sentence 1 (`forget_map_pushforward_map`) closed axiom-clean — the explicit
  STEP-1 deliverable. Sentences 2–3 closed as committed compiling tactic steps (bridge wired in +
  RHS factor split), each with a recorded defeq/contamination finding.

You are a prover agent for project 'Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge'. Current stage: prover.
Archon iteration: 265.
